questions.question: Explain the exec family of functions and list all six variants.
keyPoints: exec() replaces current program with new program in same process
Six variants: execl, execv, execle, execve, execlp, execvp
execl - list arguments, execv - array of arguments
execle/execve - pass environment variables
execlp/execvp - search in PATH
Process ID remains same after exec

questions.question: Differentiate between unnamed pipes and named pipes (FIFO).
keyPoints: Unnamed pipes created by pipe() system call, exist while file descriptors open
Named pipes (FIFO) appear as special files in filesystem
Unnamed pipes require parent-child relationship
FIFO allows unrelated processes to communicate
Both provide unidirectional communication
FIFO persists in filesystem until explicitly removed

questions.question: What is shared memory IPC? Explain its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Shared memory allows multiple processes to access common memory region
Fastest IPC - no kernel intervention for data transfer
Processes directly read/write to shared memory
Requires synchronization (semaphores) to avoid race conditions
Disadvantage: explicit synchronization needed
Disadvantage: more complex to implement correctly

questions.question: Explain semaphores as a synchronization mechanism in IPC.
keyPoints: Semaphore is a counter-based synchronization primitive
Supports atomic operations: wait() and signal()
wait() decrements counter, blocks if zero
signal() increments counter, wakes waiting processes
Used to control access to shared resources
Prevents race conditions in concurrent processes

questions.question: What are signals in UNIX? How are they used for IPC?
keyPoints: Signals are software interrupts for asynchronous notifications
Sent to process to indicate events (termination, errors, etc.)
Process can handle signals with signal handlers
Common signals: SIGKILL, SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGCHLD
Used for simple asynchronous communication
Limited for bulk data transfer

questions.question: Explain the difference between execve() and other exec functions.
keyPoints: execve() is the only system call; others are library wrappers
execve() directly takes arguments as array and environment
Other functions internally call execve()
execve() allows complete control over arguments and environment
Functions with 'e' suffix (execle, execve) pass custom environment
execve() is more efficient as it bypasses library overhead

questions.question: List and briefly explain the various IPC methods available in UNIX.
keyPoints: Pipes: unidirectional communication channel
FIFO (Named Pipes): persistent special files for unrelated processes
Message Queues: typed message transfer with queues
Shared Memory: fastest IPC, direct memory access
Semaphores: synchronization for resource access
Signals: asynchronous event notifications
Sockets: bidirectional communication, local or network

questions.question: Explain in detail the exec family of functions with their syntax and differences between each variant. Include a practical example showing how to use execvp().
keyPoints: exec() replaces current process image with new program
Six variants differ in argument passing and features
execl, execle, execlp - list format arguments
execv, execve, execvp - vector/array format
p suffix: search in PATH environment variable
e suffix: accept custom environment array
v suffix: arguments as array
l suffix: arguments as list
Only execve is system call; others are wrappers
Example code with execvp() showing argument array
Error handling and return values

questions.question: Discuss the different IPC mechanisms in UNIX/Linux. Compare them based on speed, use cases, and complexity.
keyPoints: Pipes: simple, unidirectional, parent-child only
FIFO: named pipes, unrelated processes
Message Queues: SysV/POSIX, typed messages
Shared Memory: fastest, direct access, needs sync
Semaphores: synchronization, resource control
Signals: async notifications, limited data
Sockets: most versatile, local/network
Speed comparison: Shared Memory > Pipes > Sockets > MsgQ
Use cases: pipe for simple child communication
FIFO for daemon-client, MsgQ for prioritized messages
Shared memory for high-performance data sharing
Sockets for network and complex communication
Complexity: Shared memory highest, pipes lowest

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating inter-process communication using pipes. The parent should send a message to child and child should display it.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers: unistd.h, stdio.h, string.h
Create pipe using pipe() system call
Use fork() to create child process
Parent process: close read end, write message, close write end
Child process: close write end, read message, close read end
Use read() and write() system calls
Handle errors appropriately
Show message sent from parent to child
Proper file descriptor cleanup
Explanation of flow

questions.question: Explain shared memory and semaphore concepts in System V IPC. How do they work together for synchronization?
keyPoints: Shared memory: shmget(), shmat(), shmdt(), shmctl()
Semaphores: semget(), semop(), semctl()
Shared memory creates/attaches common memory region
Processes access shared memory without kernel data copy
Semaphores provide atomic operations for synchronization
wait() operation decrements, blocks if value <= 0
signal() operation increments, wakes waiting processes
Together: semaphores protect shared memory access
Prevents race conditions and data inconsistency
Example: binary semaphore (mutex) for mutual exclusion
Process acquires semaphore before accessing shared memory
Releases after done to allow other processes access
